## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by permuting the index \(i\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials in \(P_n^{(m)}\) with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}.
\]
Give \(R_n^{(m)}\) the \(\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\)-grading in which \(x_i^{(r)}\) has degree \(1\) in the \(r\)-th component and degree \(0\) in the others. The universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the coefficients in the stable Schur expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
where \(s_\lambda\) is the Schur polynomial and \(s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(h,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\subseteq h\times r\) means a weakly decreasing sequence
\[
\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_h)
\]
with \(r\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge\rho_h\ge0\), and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_h\). If \(h=0\), only the empty partition is allowed.

The theorem proved here is:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Here the Gaussian binomial coefficient is
\[
\begin{bmatrix} r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

In particular, when \(b=0\),
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\},
\]
and if \(b\ge n-1\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First note that the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by the image of \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard facts, stated in the precise forms needed.

### Known input 1: the \(GL(1|1)\) super Hilbert specialization

Let
\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

Here
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

---

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Now let \(SR_n\) be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one bosonic and one fermionic set:
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)
\Big/
\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle .
\]
Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree.

A standard diagonal supersymmetry theorem says that for the coinvariant algebra with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets,
\[
R_n^{(k,j)},
\]
there are multiplicities \(a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}\), independent of \(k,j\), such that its graded Frobenius characteristic has the form
\[
{\rm Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\) gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_\mu a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), this is the original bosonic Schur expansion, so by uniqueness
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_\mu a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu.
\]
Therefore, for \((k,j)=(1,1)\),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

We next evaluate the \(1|1\) super Schur functions. Using the standard formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
a one-variable term \(s_\nu(q)\) is nonzero only when \(\nu=(r)\). Also, \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero only when \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip. This happens precisely when \(\lambda\) is a hook. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
Non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization, and \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\).

On the other hand, the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert formula for \(SR_n\), together with the Remmel–Wilson/Carlitz permutation identity, gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
For fixed \(w\), write \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
with the convention that the expression is \(0\) when \(b>d\). Hence (3) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

We solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
By (4),
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), contributing \(1\). Thus
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (5),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (4) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this simplifies to
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, for \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then the descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula indeed counts coefficients \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) with \(a\ge1\). This proves the claimed hook formula.

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q ,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting variable sets. Thus \(R_n^{(k,0)}=R_n^{(k)}\), and \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\). We use Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem: there are nonnegative integers \(A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Apply the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\), and define
\[
d_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu
\quad(\ell(\lambda)\le n),
\]
with \(d_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(\ell(\lambda)>n\). Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and \(P(k,0,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\}\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
\]
On the other hand, the original bosonic Schur expansion in \(k\) variables is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le k}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
since \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_k)=0\) when \(\ell(\lambda)>k\). Schur polynomials of length at most \(k\) are linearly independent, so \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for \(\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\), and \(c_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(n<\ell(\lambda)\le k\). Varying \(k\) gives \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for every \(\lambda\).

Now specialize to \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The super Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u). \tag{1}
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\); similarly, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. From (1), for a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}. \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, the only possible rows \(\nu=(r)\) giving a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). Also \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr). \tag{3}
\]

Define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (3) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q). \tag{4}
\]

We now compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) independently. Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) denotes the Carlitz \(q\)-Stirling number. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8)) Putting \(r=n-k\), this is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{5}
\]
Remmel and Wilson’s identity gives
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
\prod_{j=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}
\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{6}
\]
([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0097316515000497)) Combining (5) and (6), and writing \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), we obtain
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u). \tag{7}
\]

For fixed \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q
\]
when \(b\le d\), and is \(0\) otherwise. Hence (7) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we have
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{8}
\]

We solve (4) by induction on \(b\). For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}-1.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\), so
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (4),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (8) and the induction hypothesis, the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d\brack b}_q
-
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q. \tag{9}
\]

Finally,
\[
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (9) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed gives the stated formula for \(a\ge1\). ∎

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the corresponding diagonal coinvariant algebra with one commuting variable set and one anticommuting variable set; let \(q\) record commuting degree and \(u\) record anticommuting degree.

**Known input 1: supersymmetric universality.** The same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appear in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive, unsigned \(GL(1|1)\) super-Schur specialization.

**Known input 2: \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization.** In this convention,
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook partitions specialize to \(0\).

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\tag{1}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (1) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{2}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is \(1\) if \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

**Known input 3: Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula.** One has
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). For fixed \(w\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since \(\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2}\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
The standard Gaussian-binomial identity gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\).

We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

For \(b=0\), formula (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The only permutation with no descents is the identity \(12\cdots n\), and it has major index \(0\). Hence
\[
H_0(q)=1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
which is (5) for \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this contribution equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Thus (5) holds for all \(b\ge0\).

Comparing (2) and (5), we get
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\), with \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\). For \(b=0\), this gives \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\). Inductively, if \(C_{b-1}(q)=D_{b-1}(q)\), then the displayed equality gives \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\). Therefore
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, by the combinatorial interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the sum of the \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge \binom{d+1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
So the coefficient extraction is genuinely for \(a\ge1\).

Finally, since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in S_n\), if \(b\ge n-1\) then the condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }a\ge1\text{ whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]
This completes the proof.

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients in the Hilbert series of the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

We use the diagonal-supersymmetry Hilbert-series theorem: for the bosonic/fermionic diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), there are coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
When \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the pure-bosonic coefficients from the original Schur expansion. When \(k=j=1\), this gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
This is the Hilbert-series form of Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), so the constant term of its Hilbert series is \(1\). Since \(s_\varnothing=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For one bosonic and one fermionic variable, the positive super-Schur specialization satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. Hence non-hooks vanish. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

Next we compute \(H_b(q)\). Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=
[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1).
\]
([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

This \(q\)-Stirling formula implies the product form
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{2}
\]
Indeed, let
\[
A_{n,d}
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}.
\]
Carlitz’s insertion argument gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\tag{3}
\]
To see this, insert \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Inserting after a descent or at the final gap preserves the number of descents and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{\operatorname{des}(v)}\). Inserting before the first entry or after an ascent increases the number of descents by \(1\) and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{n-\operatorname{des}(v)-2}\). This gives exactly (3).

Now let
\[
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
with the convention that \({d\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>d\). The \(q\)-Pascal identities give
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr),
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}
=
[e+1-b]_q\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr).
\]
Thus the coefficient \(K_{n,b}\) of \(u^b\) on the right side of (2) satisfies
\[
K_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the Rhoades-Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence, with the same initial condition \(K_{1,0}=1\). Hence (2) follows.

Using (2), for \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
For \(b=0\), (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
since the unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\).

For \(b\ge 1\), fix \(w\in S_n\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
by \(q\)-Pascal. This is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Hence (5) holds.

Comparing (1) and (5),
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Since \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive when \(d\ge b+1\), because
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
This completes the proof.

**Claim.** For every \(n\ge 1\),
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Moreover, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\ w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in \operatorname{Des}(w)}i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

**Proof.** The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), so the constant term of its Hilbert series is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive degree, we immediately get
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now determine the hook coefficients. We use two standard facts about the superspace coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\). First, its Hilbert series has the universal expansion
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as in the purely bosonic case. In particular, for \(k=j=1\),
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu\) is a row, and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. Thus \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) unless \(\lambda\) is a hook. If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then the only contributing rows are \((a)\) and \((a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and let
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the superspace Schur expansion gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b(q)\). Let
\[
[r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1},\qquad [r]_q!=\prod_{i=1}^r [i]_q.
\]
The Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert-series formula says
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=
[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1),
\]
with the usual zero boundary conditions.

We rewrite this formula in permutation form. For \(0\le d\le n-1\), set
\[
A_{n,d}
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}.
\]
We claim that
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\tag{2}
\]

To prove this, insert \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Suppose
\[
\operatorname{Des}(v)=\{i_1<\cdots<i_e\},\qquad \operatorname{maj}(v)=M.
\]
If \(n\) is inserted after the descent \(i_j\), then the descent at \(i_j\) is replaced by one at \(i_j+1\), and descents to its right shift by \(1\). The number of descents remains \(e\), and the major-index increase is \(e-j+1\). Inserting \(n\) at the final gap also preserves the number of descents and gives major-index increase \(0\). Hence descent-preserving insertions contribute
\[
1+q+\cdots+q^e=[e+1]_q.
\]

If \(n\) is inserted before the first entry, then the number of descents increases to \(e+1\), the major-index increase is \(e+1\), and this is exactly canceled by the normalization change
\[
\binom{e+2}{2}-\binom{e+1}{2}=e+1.
\]
So the normalized exponent shift is \(0\).

If \(n\) is inserted after an ascent at position \(i\), and if
\[
r=\#\{h\in\operatorname{Des}(v):h\le i\},
\]
then the major-index increase is
\[
i+1+(e-r),
\]
because a new descent appears at \(i+1\), and the \(e-r\) descents to the right shift by \(1\). The number of descents increases to \(e+1\), so the normalized exponent shift is
\[
i+1+e-r-(e+1)=i-r.
\]
As the ascents are read from left to right, the numbers \(i-r\) are
\[
1,2,\ldots,n-e-2.
\]
Together with the initial gap, descent-increasing insertions contribute
\[
1+q+\cdots+q^{n-e-2}=[n-e-1]_q.
\]
Setting \(e=d\) in the descent-preserving case and \(e=d-1\) in the descent-increasing case proves (2).

Now define
\[
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u).
\]
Then
\[
P_{d,b}
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
with \(P_{d,b}=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>d\). Using the \(q\)-Pascal identities, one has
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr),
\tag{3}
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}
=
[e+1-b]_q\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr).
\tag{4}
\]

Let
\[
K_{n,b}=\sum_d A_{n,d}P_{d,b}.
\]
This is the coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Using (2), (3), and (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
K_{n,b}
&=
\sum_e A_{n-1,e}
\Bigl([e+1]_qP_{e,b}+[n-e-1]_qP_{e+1,b}\Bigr) \\
&=
\sum_e A_{n-1,e}
\Bigl([e+1-b]_q+q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q\Bigr)
\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr) \\
&=
[n-b]_q\sum_e A_{n-1,e}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr) \\
&=
[n-b]_q\bigl(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{5}
\]

On the other hand, if
\[
L_{n,b}=[u^b]\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
then
\[
L_{n,b}=[n-b]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-b).
\]
The \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
L_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(L_{n-1,b}+L_{n-1,b-1}\bigr),
\]
with the same initial and boundary conditions as \(K_{n,b}\). Hence \(K_{n,b}=L_{n,b}\) for all \(n,b\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{6}
\]

Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (6), and writing \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{7}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{8}
\]

For \(b=0\), (7) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation contributes \(1\), and all other permutations contribute exactly \(D_0(q)\). Hence (8) holds for \(b=0\).

For \(b\ge1\), fix \(w\in S_n\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (7). Thus (8) holds.

Comparing (1) and (8), we have
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Since \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives precisely
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\ w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]

The exponent is always positive when \(d\ge b+1\), since
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula indeed describes the coefficients of the nonempty hooks \((a,1^b)\).

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
for the diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(m)}\) with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables. For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\quad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For a partition \(\rho\), write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts, and say that \(\rho\) is contained in a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and largest part at most \(r\).

Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where \({r\brack s}_q\) is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with invalid values taken to be \(0\). In particular, \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for all \(a\ge1\) whenever \(b\ge n-1\). This is exactly the requested hook-shape interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge1\). We use two known inputs.

First, by diagonal supersymmetry, the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurring in the ordinary bosonic Schur-Hilbert expansion also occur in the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series of the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant ring \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), expanded in positive super-Schur functions:
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
Here \(q\) records bosonic degree and \(u\) records fermionic degree as a positive Hilbert variable; \(u\) is not a signed supertrace variable. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem is precisely the universality statement that these super-Schur coefficients are independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), the positive super-Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) if \(\lambda\) is not a hook. Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_{\varnothing}(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The constant term of the Hilbert series is \(1\), so
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Second, we use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula in tableau form, as proved via the Fields Conjectures. Murai, Rhoades, and Wilson prove the bigraded \(S_n\)-module structure of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), and Theorem 5.2 gives the required Frobenius characteristic formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027)) In the form needed here, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\,{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu.
\]
Here \(T\) is a standard Young tableau, with descent set defined so that \(i\) is a descent of \(T\) when \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\).

To forget the \(S_n\)-module structure, pair the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\). Since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\,{\rm SYT}(\mu)\), this sends \(s_\mu\) to \(f^\mu\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q.
\]
By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((U,T)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, where the recording tableau \(T=Q(w)\) satisfies
\[
{\rm Des}(T)={\rm Des}(w).
\]
Thus the preceding tableau sum becomes
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
We show that \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Hence
\[
D_0(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}
=H_0(q)-1.
\]
This matches (1), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

For \(b\ge1\), compare \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) with \(H_b(q)\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). The contribution of this \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\), using the convention that invalid Gaussian binomials vanish, is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q)\qquad(b\ge1).
\]
Together with \(D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), the polynomials \(D_b(q)\) satisfy the same triangular recurrence (1) as the \(C_b(q)\). Hence, by induction on \(b\),
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). That is,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q. \tag{3}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (3) gives
\[
C_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly the claimed count of pairs \((w,\rho)\).

The edge cases follow immediately. For \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts non-identity permutations with \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so all such hook coefficients vanish. In particular, when \(n=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients are \(0\), while \(c_{\varnothing}(1)=1\). ∎

\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]

c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

## Proof

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(q\) records bosonic degree, \(u\) records fermionic degree, and \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive super-Schur function. This is the Hilbert-series specialization of the universal super-Schur character expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Second, the superspace Frobenius formula gives, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]
This is Theorem 5.2 together with the Schur expansion in equation (2.5) of the Fields-conjecture formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))

We first record the needed one-variable super-Schur specialization.

### Lemma

In one even Hilbert variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

### Proof of the lemma

Use the positive hook-Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\mu(q)=q^r\). Also, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, i.e. has at most one box in each column; in that case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so \(\lambda'\) has at least two boxes in its second column. Removing \(\mu'=(1^r)\) removes boxes only from the first column, so \(\lambda'/\mu'\) still has two boxes in the second column. Thus no term contributes.

If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only choices of \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). These give respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

Now expand the superspace Hilbert series using the lemma:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)
+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The \((0,0)\)-bidegree of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, so
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\). Pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\) forgets the \(S_n\)-module structure, since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
By RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, and \(\operatorname{Des}(Q)=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q . \tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]
We show \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Thus
\[
D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1.
\]
By (1), \(C_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the contribution of \(w\) to \(H_b(q)\) in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q).
\]
Together with (1) and the already proved equality \(C_0=D_0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally, use the standard rectangle interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.
