## Problem Solved

Fix an integer \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables, and let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)=(a,\underbrace{1,\ldots,1}_{b\text{ times}})
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). The empty partition is denoted \(\varnothing\).

For integers \(r,s\), use the Gaussian binomial convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i}
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq s\times t\) means that \(\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_s)\) is a partition with
\[
t\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge \rho_s\ge 0,
\]
and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_s\). For \(s=0\), there is one empty partition of size \(0\).

The result proved is:

\[
\boxed{c_\varnothing(n)=1.}
\]

For every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[2mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation; the statement also records the degree-zero coefficient \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

## Proof

We use three standard Hilbert-series inputs.

1. **Super-Schur universality.** The same Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-bosonic, one-fermionic superspace specialization:
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
   \]
   where \(q\) records the ordinary bosonic degree and \(u\) is an ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable.

2. **One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)
   =
   q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook shapes vanish in this specialization.

3. **Superspace Hilbert formula.** The Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Hilbert formula, equivalently after RSK, gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
   \]

First, the degree-zero component of the coinvariant algebra is one-dimensional, and only \(s_\varnothing\) contributes to total degree zero. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set \(C_{-1}(q)=0\). Also define
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the super-Schur specialization,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from the product formula. Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
this equals
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using the standard subset form of the Gaussian binomial,
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We show that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\).

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and again write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge 1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the \(w\)-summand in formula \((2)\).

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\). There is exactly one permutation with \(d=0\), namely the identity permutation, and its contribution to \(H_0(q)\) is \(1\). This is exactly supplied by the term \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\).

Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Using
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
we get
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed combinatorial interpretation.

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula. The empty coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). When \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\), and \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible and the coefficient vanishes. For \(n=1\), the only permutation has no descents, so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\) in \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables, and let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be its universal Schur-Hilbert expansion. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[1mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[1mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \({\rm des}(w)\) is the number of descents of \(w\), \({\rm maj}(w)\) is the sum of its descent positions, and \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings: for fixed \(n\), the Hilbert series of the \((k,j)\)-bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring has an expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same Hilbert-level coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) for all \(k,j\). If the theorem is stated at the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-character level, evaluating the character at the identity gives this Hilbert-series form. Taking \(j=0\) gives the purely bosonic expansion above, and taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Let
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)/SI_n
\]
be the superspace coinvariant ring, where \(SI_n\) is generated by \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants with zero constant term. Rhoades–Wilson prove its bigraded Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form; equivalently, by the Carlitz \(q\)-Eulerian/\(q\)-Stirling identity,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{1}
\]
The quotient definition and the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula are those of Rhoades–Wilson. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763))

Now compute the one-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization. With the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, every nonempty non-hook shape vanishes. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (2) in the superspace expansion,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(b\ge 0\),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from (1). Fix \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand of (1) is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge 1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (4). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge 0.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook interpretation.

**Claim.** For fixed \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be the universal Schur expansion for the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\). Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry gives, for the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). We use the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables, so
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
Also, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur.

Second, for the one-boson, one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\), and
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(N,K)
=
{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K-1)+[K]_q{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K),
\qquad
{\rm Stir}_q(0,K)=\delta_{0,K}.
\]

We first record the required Carlitz normalization. For \(0\le b\le N\), set
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(N,N-b),
\]
and
\[
E_{N,b}
=
\sum_{w\in \mathfrak S_N}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Indeed, the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q\bigl(O_{N-1,b}+O_{N-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The same recurrence holds for \(E_{N,b}\). Insert \(N\) into a permutation \(v\in\mathfrak S_{N-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\), \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after descents of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, preserve the descent number; their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{e\brack b}_q[e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(N-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,N-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of all insertions of \(N\) into \(v\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket becomes
\[
[N-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{e+1\brack b}_q={e\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{e\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is \([N-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{N-1,b}+E_{N-1,b-1}\). The initial conditions agree, hence \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Therefore, if
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u),
\]
then
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{1}
\]

Now compute the same coefficient using supersymmetry. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, the only nonempty \(\lambda\) that survive are hooks. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
The empty partition contributes \(1\), so by (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed nonidentity \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so this equals the \(w\)-summand in (1). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, whose contribution to (1) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all } b\ge0.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus (5) becomes
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed counting formula.

The constant term of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants, and no nonempty Schur polynomial has degree \(0\), so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Also, if \(b\ge n\), then \(\ell(a,1^b)>n\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\); on the combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the required condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus the formula holds in all hook cases.

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\). Also
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\qquad
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\quad\text{if }b\ge n-1.
\]

We prove this using the refined Frobenius coefficients from diagonal supersymmetry, the one-bosonic-one-fermionic super Schur specialization, and the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem on the superspace coinvariant ring.

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Thus \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), and \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\). Diagonal supersymmetry gives nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu,
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). For the purely bosonic ring, \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure gives
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu},
\qquad f^\mu:=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|.
\]
The degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation, so
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
These are the standard diagonal-supersymmetry inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and every non-hook shape specializes to \(0\). This follows from the super Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally, the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}u^r
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-r\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is their \(C_{n,k}\)-form with \(r=n-k\). Equivalently, it matches the product form
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
\]
because
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-r+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-r+1}{2}
=
-rd+\binom r2.
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

We use the convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

For fixed \(\mu\vdash n\), let
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q):=[u^b s_\mu]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
From Murai–Rhoades–Wilson,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q):=0.
\]
Using the one-variable super Schur specialization,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

We now solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The unique standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]

Assume \(b\ge1\). For \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Using the recurrence and the induction hypothesis,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
and the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\quad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive under \(d\ge b+1\), since the sum of \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

Now pass from refined Frobenius coefficients to Hilbert coefficients:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing a second standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples \((\mu,T,U,\rho)\) with \(T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) satisfying the conditions above.

By the Robinson–Schensted correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Taking \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\), the quadruples are therefore in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d\ge b+1\ge n\) is impossible. Hence the counting set is empty, and
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad(b\ge n-1).
\]

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients determined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)
\]
for the diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(m)}\) with \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables. For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For nonnegative integers \(b,r\), a partition \(\rho\) is contained in a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. We use the convention
\[
{r\brack b}_q=0\quad\text{if }b<0\text{ or }b>r,
\]
and, for \(0\le b\le r\),
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\,
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-shape interpretation, with the additional empty-partition value recorded.

## Proof

We use two standard inputs.

**Supersymmetric universality.** The same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) from the ordinary bosonic Schur-Hilbert expansion occur in the positive Hilbert series of the one-even, one-odd superspace coinvariant specialization \(R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
Here \(q\) records the commuting degree and \(u\) records the anticommuting degree by ordinary dimension; \(u\) is not a signed supertrace variable.

**One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In this specialization,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and every nonempty non-hook Schur term vanishes.

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Therefore
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Using the two inputs above,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{1}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is the Kronecker delta.

Next use the known consequence of the Fields/Murai-Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula together with ordinary row-insertion RSK:
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{2}
\]
For \(d={\rm des}(w)\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the product is
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d\brack b}_q,
\tag{3}
\]
again with \(d={\rm des}(w)\).

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We claim that \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Indeed, fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b(q)\) from (3). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity \(12\cdots n\), with \(M=0\). It contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\), and it contributes nothing to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\). This sole missing contribution is precisely \(\delta_{b,0}\), proving (4).

Comparing (1) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)\qquad(b\ge0).
\]
Hence
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (5) gives
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) stated in the theorem.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

The edge cases follow immediately. For \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and major index \(a\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\). For \(n=1\), there is only the identity permutation, so \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. ∎

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Let \(n\ge 1\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
R_n^{(m)}
=
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle
\]
be the bosonic diagonal coinvariant algebra, where \(S_n\) acts diagonally by
\[
w\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{w(i)}^{(r)}
\]
and \(\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{S_n}_{+}\) denotes the positive-degree invariant polynomials. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficient defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
for all \(m\), with the convention that \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

The proof establishes the following exact hook interpretation. First,
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
For every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n,\\
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=\#\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i,
\]
and \(|\rho|\) is the sum of the parts of the partition \(\rho\). The notation
\(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and largest part at most \(r\). If \(r<0\), there are no such \(\rho\); if \(b=0\), the only such \(\rho\) is the empty partition. This is exactly the original hook-coefficient problem, not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Supersymmetric universality input

For \(k,j\ge 0\), let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) sets of commuting variables and \(j\) sets of anticommuting variables. Thus \(R_n^{(m,0)}=R_n^{(m)}\). We use the following known supersymmetric universality theorem: for fixed \(n\), the same nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurring in the bosonic Schur expansion also occur in the positive Hilbert-series expansion
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function and \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). The Hilbert series here is an ordinary dimension-counting series in the fermionic variables \(\mathbf u\), not a signed supertrace. Lentfer defines the bosonic–fermionic rings and proves this Hilbert-series universality; his Hilbert series is explicitly a sum of dimensions with positive \(u\)-powers. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), the relevant super Schur specializations are
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All non-hooks specialize to \(0\). This is the positive super Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed convention. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Set
\[
C_b(q):=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q):=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Using the one-even one-odd hook specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
}
\tag{1}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is the Kronecker delta.

### 2. Superspace Frobenius input and the formula for \(H_b(q)\)

We next use the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,
\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
where \(S_n\) acts diagonally and \(SI_n\) is the ideal generated by positive-degree invariants. This is \(R_n^{(1,1)}\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove the Fields Frobenius formula
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}\mathcal C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q),
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree, \(z\) tracks fermionic degree, and
\[
\mathcal C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Here \(\operatorname{SYT}(n)\) is the set of standard Young tableaux with \(n\) boxes, and \(i\) is a descent of \(T\) when \(i+1\) lies in a strictly lower row than \(i\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Rename \(z\) as \(u\), and fix \(b\ge 0\). The fermionic degree \(b\) piece corresponds to \(n-k=b\). The Hilbert series is obtained from the Frobenius characteristic by pairing with \(h_1^n\), since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\sum_{T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Use ordinary row-insertion RSK:
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)).
\]
With this convention,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus, in the previous tableau-pair sum, take \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\). We obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]
The Gaussian binomial coefficient is understood to be zero unless \(0\le b\le \operatorname{des}(w)\).

### 3. Solving the triangular recurrence

Define
\[
D_{-1}(q):=0
\]
and, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
D_b(q)
:=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{3}
\]

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
If \(d\ge 1\), the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (2).

It remains only to handle \(d=0\). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity permutation \(12\cdots n\), for which \(M=0\). Its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) in (2) is \(1\) when \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise. It contributes to no \(D_b(q)\). Therefore the missing contribution is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). This proves (3).

Now compare (1) and (3). Since \(C_{-1}(q)=D_{-1}(q)=0\), the recurrence determines \(C_b(q)\) uniquely by induction on \(b\). Hence
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\). Thus
\[
\boxed{
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{4}
\]

### 4. Converting the Gaussian binomial into partitions

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)\) means that \(\rho\) fits inside a rectangle of height \(b\) and width \(d-1-b\).

Substituting this into (4) gives
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
But
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
This is the desired combinatorial interpretation.

The exponent is always positive in the nonempty hook case: if \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), then \(\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]

Finally, the edge cases agree with the statement:

- \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), because the degree-zero component is spanned by \(1\).
- If \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\). Hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts non-identity permutations \(w\in S_n\) with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\), since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) but the formula requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\).

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). In the universal Schur expansion
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
we have \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Moreover, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2pt]
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\\[2pt]
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=\#\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

### Proof

Let \(SR_n\) denote the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant algebra
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)\big/ I_n,
\]
where \(I_n\) is the ideal generated by the positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariants, with \(S_n\) acting diagonally. Let \(q\) track \(x\)-degree and \(u\) track \(\theta\)-degree.

We use the standard supersymmetric universality theorem for diagonal coinvariants: the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda} c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where the positive super Schur specialization is
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), this specialization is especially simple. We have
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for a nonempty hook \((a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All non-hooks specialize to \(0\). Indeed, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) has at most one cell in each column. That happens precisely for hook shapes, with the two contributing rows \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\).

Since the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is one-dimensional, only \(s_\varnothing=1\) contributes to the constant term, so
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_b(q):=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q):=0,
\]
and let
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the super Schur expansion gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

Next we compute \(H_b(q)\). The graded Frobenius formula for \(SR_n\) gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{b=0}^{n-1}
u^b
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
Pairing with \(h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu\) converts Frobenius characteristic to Hilbert series. Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Under ordinary RSK, \(w\leftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w))\), the recording tableau satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus the previous tableau sum becomes
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_{-1}(q):=0,
\]
and for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
D_b(q)
:=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\).

If \(d=0\), then \(w=12\cdots n\). Its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is \(1\) for \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise, and it contributes to no \(D_b(q)\). This accounts for the term \(\delta_{b,0}\).

Now assume \(d\ge 1\). For \(b=0\), the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_0(q)+q^{-1}D_{-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^M\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\0\end{bmatrix}_q=q^M
=
q^M\begin{bmatrix}d\\0\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly its contribution to \(H_0(q)\).

For \(b\ge 1\), using the convention that
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{if }s<0\text{ or }s>r,
\]
the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is precisely the \(w\)-summand in \((2)\). Hence \((3)\) holds.

Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}(q)=D_{-1}(q)=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\tag{4}
\]

Finally, for \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into \((4)\) yields
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonempty-hook case: if \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction is consistent with \(a\ge 1\). This proves the claim.

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For \(0\le b\le r\), use
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r-b\). Also set \({r\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>r\).

Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficient of the empty partition is
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\,
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

**Proof.** Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. Diagonal supersymmetry gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking ordinary Hilbert series means taking dimensions of the \(S_n\)-modules, so \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\) is replaced by \(f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\). Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n}\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the coefficient in parentheses is exactly the bosonic coefficient \(c_\lambda(n)\). Therefore the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurs for the one-even, one-odd specialization \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

We next evaluate \(s_\lambda(q/u)\). By the hook-Schur identity,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\). Also \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

Thus
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1.
\]
If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\), and the only contributing \(\nu=(r)\) are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). They give
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
If \(\lambda\) is nonempty and not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so for every \(r\), the skew shape \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) has at least two cells in some column; hence it is not a horizontal strip. Therefore \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) for nonhook \(\lambda\).

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Now we compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. The superspace Frobenius formula of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k}.
\]
The Wilson/Haglund–Rhoades–Shimozono Schur expansion says that for \(b=n-k\),
\[
C_{n,n-b}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{\lambda\vdash n}\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\lambda)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q\,s_\lambda(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking dimensions replaces \(s_\lambda(\mathbf z)\) by \(f^\lambda=\# {\rm SYT}(\lambda)\). Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, with
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\); this accounts for the extra \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus (5) holds.

Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
A_b(q)=B_b(q)\qquad(b\ge0).
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into the last formula gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). In the stable Schur expansion
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
the empty-shape coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Moreover, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|\), \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}}i\), and \(\rho\subseteq r\times s\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(r\) parts, each at most \(s\).

**Proof.** Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of variables. By Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem, there are coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking Hilbert series is pairing the Frobenius characteristic in the \(\mathbf z\)-variables with
\[
(h_1)^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so uniqueness of the Schur expansion identifies
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]
Therefore, for the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\),
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
This is the required coefficient-identification step. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants. Since \(s_\varnothing=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive degree, the constant term of the Hilbert series is exactly \(c_\varnothing(n)\). Thus
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now compute the one-even/one-odd specialization. The positive super Schur specialization satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
With one ordinary variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row. With one variable \(u\), the skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\) is nonzero only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip. Equivalently, \(\lambda/\nu\) is a vertical strip. Therefore nonzero terms occur only when \(\lambda\) is a hook. For a nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only contributing rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), with \((a-1)=\varnothing\) when \(a=1\). Hence
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and non-hooks specialize to \(0\).

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q):=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using the specialization above,
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove the bigraded Frobenius formula
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{\,n-k} C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q),
\]
where
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\) gives the fermionic degree \(b\) part. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027)) Pairing with \((h_1)^n\) gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(n)}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Under ordinary RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
For each fixed recording tableau \(Q=T\), there are \(f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}\) choices of insertion tableau \(P\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\tag{2}
\]

Now define \(D_{-1}(q):=0\), and for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q):=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). If \(d=0\), then \(w=12\cdots n\), contributing \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\); it contributes to no \(D_b\). This accounts for \(\delta_{b,0}\).

Assume \(d\ge1\). For \(b=0\), the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_0(q)\) is \(q^M\), matching its contribution to \(H_0(q)\). For \(b\ge1\), using the convention that Gaussian binomial coefficients vanish when the lower index is outside the allowed range, the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (2). Hence (3) holds.

Comparing (1) and (3), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Thus
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\tag{4}
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\qquad(d\ge b+1).
\]
Substituting this into (4) yields
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed formula for \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\).

The exponent is always positive in the nonempty-hook case: if \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction is consistent with \(a\ge1\), completing the proof.

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let
\[
R_n^{(m)}
=
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables, where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by \(w\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{w(i)}^{(r)}\), and where the Hilbert series is multigraded by the \(m\) variable sets:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m\ge 0}
\dim (R_n^{(m)})_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}
q_1^{i_1}\cdots q_m^{i_m}.
\]
Suppose the universal Schur expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
with coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in \mathfrak S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(r,b\), define the Gaussian binomial coefficient by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^b \frac{1-q^{r-b+i}}{1-q^i}
\quad\text{if }0\le b\le r,
\]
and set it equal to \(0\) otherwise. A partition \(\rho\) fits inside a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); \(|\rho|\) denotes its number of boxes.

Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\]
This proves the original hook-coefficient problem exactly; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Supersymmetric universality input

We use the standard bosonic-fermionic extension of the diagonal coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the quotient with \(k\) commuting, or bosonic, variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting, or fermionic, variable sets, modulo the ideal generated by positive-degree diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants. Its Hilbert series is graded by bosonic variables \(\mathbf q=(q_1,\ldots,q_k)\) and fermionic variables \(\mathbf u=(u_1,\ldots,u_j)\).

The needed universality theorem says that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function and the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the same universal coefficients as in the purely bosonic case. Setting \(j=0\) gives
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
so one recovers the original expansion for \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\).

The fermionic variables here are ordinary positive Hilbert variables: they count dimensions of fermionic-degree pieces. They are not signed supertrace variables.

Since the constant term of every coinvariant Hilbert series is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur or super Schur function has positive total degree, the coefficient of \(s_\varnothing\) is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

### 2. The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the triangular relation

Now specialize to one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\). The positive super Schur specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hooks vanish in this one-boson, one-fermion specialization.

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

### 3. The superspace Hilbert formula

Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\) be the superspace coinvariant ring with one bosonic variable set and one fermionic variable set.

For a standard Young tableau \(T\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i: i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\},
\]
\[
d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i.
\]
The Fields/Murai-Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula may be written as
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)\,
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\tag{2}
\]

Taking the ordinary Hilbert series replaces \(s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}\) by the number of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u),
\]
where \(f^\mu\) is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\).

By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in \mathfrak S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and the recording tableau satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus the Hilbert series becomes
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Extract the coefficient of \(u^b\). For fixed \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}.
\]
Therefore
\[
[u^b]\left(
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
\right)
=
q^M
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we get
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{4}
\]

### 4. Solving the triangular recurrence

Define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\).

We prove that \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) satisfies the same recurrence as \(C_b^{(n)}(q)\). The identity permutation has \(d(w)=0\) and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\). Its contribution to \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\), and it contributes to neither \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) nor \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\).

Now fix a nonidentity permutation \(w\), so \(d=d(w)\ge 1\), and let \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Its contribution to \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
the combined contribution is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) from (4).

This also handles \(b=0\): then \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\), and every nonidentity permutation contributes \(q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}\) to \(D_0^{(n)}(q)\), while the identity permutation is accounted for by the \(\delta_{0,0}\) term.

Thus
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (5) with the triangular relation (1), and using the initial conditions
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
we conclude recursively that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{6}
\]

### 5. Extracting the coefficient interpretation

The Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
In (6), \(r=d(w)-1\). Therefore
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
This is why the rectangle has \(b\) rows and \(d(w)-1-b\) columns.

Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula:

- If \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs. Hence
  \[
  c_{(a)}(n)
  =
  \#\{w\in\mathfrak S_n:d(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
  \]
  The identity permutation is excluded here and is exactly the source of \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), so the condition \(d(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- If \(n=1\), the only permutation is the identity, so
  \[
  c_\varnothing(1)=1
  \]
  and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

This completes the combinatorial interpretation of the hook coefficients.

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in\mathfrak S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad 
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and, for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in\mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\[2pt]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\[2pt]
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le b\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.
